title: Training an Autoencoder
concept: Autoencoders train by comparing the reconstructed output to the original input and minimizing reconstruction error, with no labels required.

exercises.prompt: An autoencoder tries to copy its input to its output. What might it learn along the way?

options.text: A compressed, useful summary of the data
The exact labels for each input
A random guess with no pattern

exercises.explain: By squeezing the input down and rebuilding it, the autoencoder is forced to learn the most important patterns — that's the real prize!

cards.title: Copy Me... But Smaller!
cards.body: Imagine tracing a picture from memory after only seeing a tiny blurry thumbnail of it. That's basically what an autoencoder does every single training step — and it gets better and better at it!

cards.title: How Training Works
cards.body: An autoencoder is trained by feeding it an input, letting it squeeze that input down and rebuild it, then comparing the rebuilt output to the original input. The difference between them is called the 'reconstruction error.' The network adjusts itself to shrink this error — and no labels are needed, because the input IS the answer key!

cards.title: The Forgetful Artist
cards.body: Picture an artist who glances at a painting, closes their eyes, then tries to repaint it from memory. Afterward, they compare their version to the original and note what they got wrong. Next time, they remember better. That's exactly how an autoencoder trains — glance, guess, compare, improve!

cards.title: Training on Digits
cards.body: Feed the autoencoder a picture of the number '7'. It compresses it, then rebuilds it. If the rebuilt '7' looks blurry or wrong, the error is high, so the network tweaks itself. After thousands of digits, its rebuilt '7's start looking crisp and correct — all without ever being told 'this is a 7'!

cards.title: Myth: It Needs Labeled Answers
cards.body: Nope! Unlike many AI models that need humans to label data (like tagging 'cat' or 'dog'), an autoencoder only needs the original input. It compares its own output to that same input — making it a self-supervised learner.

cards.title: Supervised vs. Autoencoder Training
cards.body: See how autoencoders skip the need for human-made labels by using the input itself as the target.

left.text: Needs labeled data: input + correct answer given by humans

right.text: Needs only input: compares output to the same input, no labels needed

exercises.prompt: What does an autoencoder compare its output to during training?
exercises.explain: The autoencoder rebuilds the input and checks how close its rebuild is to the original — no external label required.

exercises.prompt: Autoencoders require labeled training data to learn.
exercises.explain: False! Autoencoders use the input itself as the target, so they don't need human-provided labels.

exercises.sentence: During training, an autoencoder tries to minimize the ___ between its output and the original input.
exercises.explain: The gap between the rebuilt output and the original input is called the reconstruction error — shrinking it is the whole goal of training.

exercises.prompt: Which of these best describes why autoencoders don't need labels?

options.text: Because the input itself acts as the target to compare against
Because they only work on images

exercises.explain: Since the goal is to rebuild the same input, the input doubles as both the training data and the answer key — no separate label needed.

exercises.prompt: A student says: 'Autoencoders are trained by comparing their rebuilt output to a human-written label for each input.' What's wrong with this statement?

options.text: Autoencoders compare output to the original input, not a human-written label
Autoencoders don't compare anything during training
Autoencoders only train on labeled text, never images

exercises.explain: The key idea is self-supervision: the autoencoder measures reconstruction error between its output and the original input — no human labels involved at all.
